paragraphs.text: EST
Publishers of online content and services INTERNET REGIES

The gesture and the NIS bring together the main French online media (TV, radio, press, pure players) and their advertising companies. They defend an environment that preserves the ecosystem financing information, supports pluralism and cultural diversity and ensures access to open services for all.

section_path: Contribution of gesture and NIS
Response to the European Commission’s public consultation on the EPR

paragraphs.text: They are also places for exchange where members share their feedback, in particular on the implementation of the new regulations. Together, they promote a framework that respects consumers and protects the sustainability of media business models.

section_path: Contribution of gesture and NIS
Response to the European Commission’s public consultation on the EPR

paragraphs.text: With extensive experience in the application of major European consumer and user protection texts (GDPR, ePrivacy, DMA, etc.), the gesture and NIS have a concrete backsliding on their impact in the media sector. These texts have profoundly structured the digital ecosystem. Their ownership requires time to grasp the subtilities, to confront them with practice and to ensure that they are applied consistently and securely for both actors and users.

section_path: Contribution of gesture and NIS
Response to the European Commission’s public consultation on the EPR

paragraphs.text: The Digital Fairness Act project is based on a legitimate intention, but the logic followed is questionable. Europe already has a dense and coherent framework (GDPR, DSA, DMA, UCPD, CRD, AI Act) that regulates transparency, fairness and consumer protection. The Fitness Check published by the Commission in 2024 recognises that the corpus is a strong foundation and that the differences observed are mainly due to difficulties of application or interpretation, and not to a legal vacuum. The main need is therefore to consolidate and harmonise and not to add a new floor to the existing framework.

section_path: Contribution of gesture and NIS
Response to the European Commission’s public consultation on the EPR

paragraphs.text: Furthermore, several key concepts of the project, such as ‘digital vulnerability’ or ‘fairness by design’, are not objectionable. Their inherently subjective nature would lead, in practice, to implementation on a case-by-case basis which cannot be industrialised, with many hindsight effects (individual identification of ‘vulnerabilities’, varying criteria according to context, uncertainty over evidence and control), differences in interpretation between Member States and, ultimately, considerable legal uncertainty for businesses.

section_path: Contribution of gesture and NIS
Response to the European Commission’s public consultation on the EPR

paragraphs.text: The experience of the GDPR has shown that when concepts are poorly defined or too broad, they lead to uncertainty, differences in application and litigation.

section_path: Contribution of gesture and NIS
Response to the European Commission’s public consultation on the EPR

evidence_quotes.quote: how difficult it is to regulate interfaces and user paths.

paragraphs.text: In particular, it highlights how difficult it is to regulate interfaces and user paths. The current consent collection interfaces primarily respond to an accumulation of legal, regulatory and doctrinal injunctions. In practice, users have very little grasp of it.

section_path: Contribution of gesture and NIS
Response to the European Commission’s public consultation on the EPR

Response to the European Commission’s public consultation on the EPR

paragraphs.text: Publishers of online content and services REGIES INTERNET

section_path: Contribution of gesture and NIS
Response to the European Commission’s public consultation on the EPR

paragraphs.text: The proportion of users performing fine-tuning granular settings by purpose in CMP is marginal or non-existent1.

section_path: Contribution of gesture and NIS
Response to the European Commission’s public consultation on the EPR

evidence_quotes.quote: too broad reading of dark patterns

paragraphs.text: This is why we want to 'simplify' and combat consent fatigue after it has become too complex. Indeed, excessive injunctions and too broad reading of dark patterns do not always serve the user. The failure of the Cookie Pledge initiative is a good illustration of this. A posteriori simplification of an already dense framework is almost impossible. Advanced remedies such as the delegation of consent to the browser provide neither transparency nor real understanding, in addition to being potentially harmful to competition. An approach based on the empowerment of actors is preferable to the stacking of rules which, under the guise of fighting dark patterns, end up being completely counter-productive for users, and very harmful to businesses.

section_path: Contribution of gesture and NIS
Response to the European Commission’s public consultation on the EPR

evidence_quotes.quote: pixel consent pathways in emails, stacking rules and constraints makes implementation almost impossible.
no clear, proportionate and truly operational pattern could be stopped.

paragraphs.text: In some cases, such as pixel consent pathways in emails, stacking rules and constraints makes implementation almost impossible. This is evidenced by more than three years of exchanges with the CNIL during which no clear, proportionate and truly operational pattern could be stopped. The relationship between marketing rules and the GDPR/ePrivacy interpretation of pixel consent is problematic. The gesture and the NIS are at the disposal of the Commission to share their detailed work on this topic, which is particularly illustrative of the issues that might arise in the context of the EPR.

section_path: Contribution of gesture and NIS
Response to the European Commission’s public consultation on the EPR

paragraphs.text: More generally, the focus on dark patterns in the context of the EPR should not unduly broaden the scope of the law in commercial design. Care must be taken to ensure that the analysis of dark patterns does not equate legitimate marketing with an unfair practice. Whereas, as a matter of principle, the commercial message and incentive to act are part of a company's legitimate economic expression. Otherwise, over-regulation would blur the message for users, complicate the application of the law and undermine the plurality of business models.

section_path: Contribution of gesture and NIS
Response to the European Commission’s public consultation on the EPR

paragraphs.text: The reflections in the gesture and the NIS on the different consent interfaces are particularly illustrative of these challenges.

section_path: ILLUSTRATIVE FOCUS: CMP AND COOKIE WALL

paragraphs.text: For example, we have been working with our members for years on how to collect and manage consent, so that they are both fully protective for users and sustainable for publishers' business models.

section_path: ILLUSTRATIVE FOCUS: CMP AND COOKIE WALL

paragraphs.text: Seven years after the entry into force of the GDPR, however, there is still no operational certainty or legal certainty for publishers. Today, publishers apply strict requirements through their platforms for managing the

paragraphs.text: consent (CMP), to comply with the GDPR and the ePrivacy Directive, as well as with the legal guidance and case-law which, over time, have progressively clarified the level of supervision of those arrangements.

section_path: ILLUSTRATIVE FOCUS: CMP AND COOKIE WALL

evidence_quotes.quote: Those interfaces, which appear to be protective, encroach on the user's decision in a logic that focuses exclusively on privacy, without weighing up against the other fundamental rights and freedoms concerned.

paragraphs.text: Those interfaces, which appear to be protective, encroach on the user's decision in a logic that focuses exclusively on privacy, without weighing up against the other fundamental rights and freedoms concerned. However, the rate of consent directly determines the economic capacity of the media to finance their content, maintain free access and ensure pluralism of information.

section_path: ILLUSTRATIVE FOCUS: CMP AND COOKIE WALL

paragraphs.text: Faced with this situation, several publishers chose to put in place mechanisms of Pay or Consent, backed up in their strategies by a favourable legal context, although still to be clarified.

section_path: ILLUSTRATIVE FOCUS: CMP AND COOKIE WALL

paragraphs.text: The purpose of these devices is to enable users, in accordance with their preferences, to choose between several ways of accessing a service, in order to satisfy:

section_path: ILLUSTRATIVE FOCUS: CMP AND COOKIE WALL

evidence_quotes.quote: Users wishing to refuse the deposit or reading of cookies and who may then be offered an alternative route of access without cookies other than those strictly necessary for the operation of the service.

paragraphs.text: Users wishing to refuse the deposit or reading of cookies and who may then be offered an alternative route of access without cookies other than those strictly necessary for the operation of the service.

section_path: ILLUSTRATIVE FOCUS: CMP AND COOKIE WALL

evidence_quotes.quote: Users accepting advertising consideration or cookies

paragraphs.text: Users accepting advertising consideration or cookies, who will be able to choose this route to access a service while enjoying of course all the rights and safeguards under the GDPR.

section_path: ILLUSTRATIVE FOCUS: CMP AND COOKIE WALL

paragraphs.text: These schemes have been widely adopted in recent years and have undoubtedly played a role in raising awareness among users about the business model of media sites.

section_path: ILLUSTRATIVE FOCUS: CMP AND COOKIE WALL

paragraphs.text: Several of the practices highlighted in the Fitness Check which guide this public consultation could, if interpreted too broadly, undermine this type of scheme. Particular attention should be paid to ensuring that:

section_path: ILLUSTRATIVE FOCUS: CMP AND COOKIE WALL

paragraphs.text: the fight against dark patterns does not equate a clear choice between advertising and paid access with undue commercial pressure.

section_path: ILLUSTRATIVE FOCUS: CMP AND COOKIE WALL

paragraphs.text: the concept of digital vulnerability does not lead to the presumption that consent to personalised advertising is never free in a free service.

section_path: ILLUSTRATIVE FOCUS: CMP AND COOKIE WALL

evidence_quotes.quote: interface pathways that explain the business model and offer distinct and lawful options

paragraphs.text: design neutrality requirements do not prohibit interface pathways that explain the business model and offer distinct and lawful options

section_path: ILLUSTRATIVE FOCUS: CMP AND COOKIE WALL

evidence_quotes.quote: In particular, personalised advertising should not be stigmatised as a matter of principle.

paragraphs.text: In particular, personalised advertising should not be stigmatised as a matter of principle. When framed, based on consent and implemented in a transparent environment, it provides legitimate and proportionate funding leverage.

section_path: ILLUSTRATIVE FOCUS: CMP AND COOKIE WALL

paragraphs.text: The findings of the Fitness Check tend to equate personalised advertising with an exploitation of consumer vulnerabilities, without distinguishing abusive practices from lawful models, which are already largely regulated. This approach is problematic because it

section_path: ILLUSTRATIVE FOCUS: CMP AND COOKIE WALL

paragraphs.text: Est =

section_path: ILLUSTRATIVE FOCUS: CMP AND COOKIE WALL

paragraphs.text: Publishers of online content and services INTERNET REGIES

section_path: ILLUSTRATIVE FOCUS: CMP AND COOKIE WALL

paragraphs.text: once again stigmatises targeted advertising and risks invalidating advertising as a matter of principle, including targeting based on sound legal bases that respect individuals' rights.

section_path: ILLUSTRATIVE FOCUS: CMP AND COOKIE WALL

paragraphs.text: The Fitness Check does not assess the actual impact of such advertisements on consumers, nor the benefits that their relevance may have on the economic sustainability of the media or on the financing of online services2. Nor does it demonstrate the existence of an exploitation of the vulnerability of users in this context. The report also points out that ‘Mystery shoppers in the Commission’s 2022 dark patterns study were not able to identify significant cases of vulnerability farms in personalised advertising, prices, ranking and recommendations’.

section_path: ILLUSTRATIVE FOCUS: CMP AND COOKIE WALL

paragraphs.text: Gesture and NIS call for a pragmatic and proportionate approach. Europe would benefit from greater clarity of existing law, convergence of national interpretations and consistency between DSA, DMA, GDPR, ePrivacy and consumer law. Digital fairness is built by stable law, consultation between actors and recognition of the diversity of business models.

section_path: ILLUSTRATIVE FOCUS: CMP AND COOKIE WALL

evidence_quotes.quote: restricting advertising targeting would limit the valuation of their inventories and lead to an estimated drop in revenues between 30 & 45 %

paragraphs.text: 2 For digital publishers, restricting advertising targeting would limit the valuation of their inventories and lead to an estimated drop in revenues between 30 & 45 % (source: Epub Observatory 34° Edition – Oliver Wyman, page 28)
